audit_meta.verification_method: single-file code review (no Task-agent delegation; 4.5 gate n/a)
audit_meta.note: Re-audit after merging PR #14 (audit), #15 (OBS-002, ARCH-011 doc), #16 (remaining 8). Same catalog_hash; same profile.

evidence: 13 tools all snake_case, one consistent convention (server.py:737-963): list_cantons, get_school_holidays, check_date, ...
No spaces/dots/brackets/unicode in any tool name
Descriptions carry use-case context, e.g. check_date: 'Can we schedule the parents' evening on that Thursday?' (server.py:830-834)
All 13 tools now carry a structured <use_case> tag; 3 also carry <important_notes> (server.py, verified: grep -c '<use_case>' == 13)
Regression test asserts every registered tool description exposes <use_case> (tests/test_tools.py::test_all_tools_carry_use_case_tag)
Similar tools remain differentiated in prose (get_local vs get_public/get_school)
HolidayListResponse.match_type: Literal['exact','fuzzy','none'] added (models.py)
_resolve_locality returns the match_type; a single prefix hit is 'fuzzy' and the note flags it; no match is 'none' (server.py)
Tests cover exact / fuzzy (prefix) / none (tests/test_local.py)
Tool handlers take only ctx: Context; no direct request access (server.py:116-117, _client(ctx))
Dual transport selected via MCP_TRANSPORT env through Settings (settings.py:21, __main__.py:41-60)
Config is a pydantic-settings object, not module globals (settings.py:18-36)
Transport-agnostic op_* functions carry all logic; tool wrappers are thin (server.py:220-711)
Single shared lifespan for all transports (server.py:78-88)
No API keys/passwords/tokens anywhere: both upstreams are keyless (client.py, constants.py)
.gitignore excludes .env (.gitignore:12)
No os.environ default secrets; config via pydantic-settings (settings.py)
gaps: No dedicated gitleaks/trufflehog CI step and no .env.example — both moot here (the server holds no secrets at all), CI runs pip-audit instead.

evidence: 13 tools, rationale documented in the module docstring (server.py:9-12) — within the 15-20 budget with Phase-2 headroom
No 1:1 REST mapping: check_date and holidays_bundle aggregate the two upstreams (server.py:451-456, 687-692)
Anchor query ('is date X a holiday in canton Y') answered in one check_date call
Aggregating tools parallelise with asyncio.gather (server.py:453-456 check_date, 692 holidays_bundle)
Tools return self-contained results (full HolidayPeriod objects), not IDs for follow-up calls (models.py:59-86)
Every response carries source + provenance so the LLM can explain the origin (models.py:17-28)
Two primitives: 13 Tools + 1 Resource holidays://{canton}/{year} (server.py:966-984)
Resource URI schema is consistent and documented (README 'Resources' section, server.py:966-971)
All 13 tools share the explicit _RO annotation block: readOnlyHint/destructiveHint/idempotentHint/openWorldHint (server.py:95-100, applied at every @mcp.tool(annotations=_RO))
openWorldHint: true is correct — every tool reaches an external HTTP source
Annotations overview documented in README ('MCP Primitives') and docs/roadmap.md
Doc drift resolved: docs/security.md and docs/roadmap.md now say 13 tools / v0.5.0 (verified: no '10 tools' / 'v0.2.0' remaining)
All mandatory top-level files + src-layout + tests + workflows present; README.de.md mirrors README.md
gaps: 13 tools still live in a single ~1109-line server.py; the catalogue asks for a tools/ package split (file-per-group) above 5 tools, and no README justification for the single-file layout is documented. This is the sole remaining ARCH-011 gap.

evidence: protocolVersion pinned explicitly: MCP_PROTOCOL_VERSION = '2025-06-18' (constants.py:21), surfaced in source_status
CHANGELOG.md in Keep-a-Changelog format present
README has an 'MCP Protocol Version' section; Dependabot opens weekly SDK/action PRs (.github/dependabot.yml)
gaps: roadmap.md version marker (v0.2.0) is stale vs pyproject 0.5.0 — cosmetic, does not affect the pinned wire version.

evidence: Every response envelope carries a source string with licence: OpenHolidays 'CC BY 4.0', Nager.Date 'MIT' (constants.py:29-36, models.py:20)
README 'Data Sources' section and docs/network-egress.md list both sources with licences
Per-response provenance (live_api/cached/degraded) preserved, not only a global note (models.py:21-24)
Attribution text names source + licence + notes the unofficial aggregation (CC BY modification hint)
docs/security.md documents a classification table per data type; highest level = Oeffentlich (public)
Explicitly states no PII / no ISDS / no DSG processing record required in Phase 1
Aggregation note addresses re-identification: no personal micro-data, so no k-anonymity concern
Upstream failures are caught and returned as a degraded response envelope (a valid tool result, not a JSON-RPC error) with a user-safe note (server.py:198-210, client.py UpstreamError)
Validation failures raise ValueError → surfaced as an execution error by FastMCP (server.py:150-153)
test_resilience.py covers the degraded/execution-error path; test_tools.py covers input-validation rejection
This SDK (mcp 1.28.1) has no mask_error_details flag; a _safe_tool decorator wraps all 13 tools (server.py, verified: grep -c '@_safe_tool' == 13)
Deliberate ValueError messages pass through; every other exception is logged to stderr only and replaced with a generic message — tracebacks never reach the LLM
Two regression tests: masks an unexpected KeyError, passes a validation ValueError (tests/test_tools.py)
Per-call correlation id + tool name bound via contextvars in the _safe_tool wrapper; the formatter emits 'tool=<name> cid=<id>' on every line while a call runs (logging_setup.py, server.py)
Tests cover bind/unbind and cid stability within a call (tests/test_logging.py)
Structured logfmt output on stderr, multiple severities in use
gaps: Deliberate deviation (documented in CHANGELOG): the recommended structlog/loguru dependency was not added; the existing logfmt formatter + contextvars binding is the accepted equivalent. session_id is out of scope (no auth / stdio-first).

evidence: Logging handler is pinned to sys.stderr (logging_setup.py:37) — stdout stays clean for the stdio JSON-RPC channel
No print() statements anywhere in src/ (verified: grep -rn 'print(' src/ → none)
Same logging config runs for both transports, no transport-dependent switch (logging_setup.py, __main__.py)
Unit tests are respx-mocked and offline (conftest.py stubs DNS; test_tools/test_resilience/test_feed/test_ical/test_local)
test_live.py exists and is marked pytestmark = pytest.mark.live
Marker registered in pyproject.toml ([tool.pytest.ini_options] markers = ['live: ...'])
CI runs pytest -m 'not live' (ci.yml); a separate live-tests.yml workflow exists for live runs
gaps: Live tests cover a representative subset of tools rather than one live test per each of the 13 tools.

evidence: README.md has all mandatory sections incl. Overview/Features, Installation, Tools, Configuration, Safety & Limits, Architecture (ASCII diagram), Known Limitations, License
README.de.md mirrors the same top-level sections (bilingual)
CHANGELOG.md in Keep-a-Changelog format; CONTRIBUTING.md + CONTRIBUTING.de.md present (bilingual)
README 'Lifecycle Phase' section declares Phase 1 (read-only); docs/roadmap.md details Phase 1/2/3 with transition prerequisites
Phase matches annotations: all tools readOnlyHint/destructiveHint:false, no write/destructive tools
Phase-1→2 prerequisites listed incl. the completed audit run
gaps: roadmap.md is stale ('active (v0.2.0)', 'all 10 tools') vs the current 0.5.0 / 13 tools — the phase itself is correct, only the version/count markers drifted.

evidence: docs/scaling.md documents the three deployment tiers and, for multi-instance HTTP, concrete sticky-session configs keyed on Mcp-Session-Id (nginx hash, Traefik, Kubernetes ingress)
The only cross-instance state (MCP session) and its affinity requirement are made explicit; single-instance (the default) needs no config
Linked from README (both languages)
gaps: Affinity is delegated to the operator's LB via documented config; no committed sticky-session manifest or automated failover test in CI (single-instance default).

evidence: Lifespan defined with @asynccontextmanager (server.py:78-85)
FastMCP('swiss-holidays-mcp', lifespan=lifespan) (server.py:88)
HTTP client closed in the finally block (server.py:82-85); one shared httpx.AsyncClient + cache, never per tool-call (client.py:57-63, SDK-001 comments)
pydantic>=2.7 in dependencies (pyproject.toml)
Every tool has an explicit BaseModel return annotation (server.py:737-963, models.py)
Consistent envelope with source/provenance/retrieved_at + count on list responses (models.py:17-28, 97-99)
Literal types for enumerable values: Provenance, kind, scope, level (models.py:14, 63-69)
ctx.report_progress emitted at milestones in the network-bound ops: op_check_date and op_holidays_bundle (before/after the gather), op_source_status (per probe) (server.py, via the _report helper)
ctx threaded through all five tool/resource call sites; a no-op without ctx or a client progress token
Test asserts op_check_date emits 0->1 progress with a fake ctx (tests/test_tools.py)
gaps: Body logging still uses the stdlib logger rather than ctx.info(); deliberate to keep stderr discipline (OBS-004).

evidence: HTTP path builds the Starlette app in __main__._build_http_app and attaches CORSMiddleware that exposes AND allows Mcp-Session-Id (__main__.py)
allow_origins is Settings.cors_origin_list — loopback origins + MCP_CORS_ORIGINS extras, never a wildcard (settings.py)
transport_security allowed_origins reuses the same list; tests cover a preflight exposing Mcp-Session-Id and an off-list origin being refused (tests/test_http_cors.py)
HTTPS scheme enforced before every request (guard.py:38-40)
Resolved IP checked against a blocklist: private/loopback/link-local/multicast/reserved/unspecified (guard.py:49-63)
Cloud-metadata IPs explicitly blocked incl. IPv6 fd00:ec2::254 (guard.py:26, 49-51)
guard.assert_host_allowed + assert_resolved_ip_safe called before the socket opens (client.py:117-118); follow_redirects=False (client.py:62)
DNS resolved once in the guard before the request; TLS validates the original hostname; two-host frozen allow-list bounds exposure (guard.py, constants.py)

Network-layer control now shipped (deploy/cilium-egress-fqdn.yaml toFQDNs) — its DNS proxy is the authority on reachable IPs, robustly closing the rebinding window at deploy time; documented in docs/network-egress.md
gaps: Client-side DNS pinning (reusing the guard's resolved IP for the TCP connection, TOCTOU-free) is still NOT implemented in code — httpx re-resolves at connect. Deliberate: an IP-with-overridden-SNI is fragile and breaks behind an HTTP proxy. Mitigated, not eliminated, at the code layer; the network layer is the robust closure.

evidence: Default transport is stdio when no env var is set (settings.py:21, __main__.py:58-60)
SSE/HTTP only activates via explicit MCP_TRANSPORT (settings.py:29-31)
README documents the local stdio + Claude Desktop use-case primarily and the cloud case separately with security notes
Dockerfile creates and switches to an unprivileged user, uid 10001 (>= 10000): useradd --uid 10001 appuser; USER appuser (Dockerfile)
Multi-stage build keeps the runtime image lean
No file-write tools, so no writable volume mounts are required
gaps: No Kubernetes securityContext manifest (readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities.drop, seccomp) — not applicable to the documented local-stdio/container deployment; would be the next step for a K8s target.

evidence: No pre/postinstall hooks; standard pip/uvx install (pyproject.toml, README Installation)
README shows the full install command transparently
PyPI publish uses OIDC Trusted Publisher (publish.yml: environment pypi, permissions id-token: write); MCP registry publish also via GitHub OIDC
auth_model is none: the server has no authentication and no user-identity concept (settings.py, docs/security.md 'no built-in authentication')
README/docs mandate an authenticating reverse proxy in front for any network deployment
gaps: The check's user_id:session_id cryptographic binding presupposes an auth model this server intentionally does not have; session/user binding is delegated to the reverse proxy. Not applicable to the current profile.

evidence: Public Open Data profile: no API keys needed (both upstreams keyless) — Stufe 1 is acceptable and documented
Secret handling documented in docs/security.md 'Supply chain' (equivalent of docs/secret-management.md)
Container image contains no secrets in any layer (no ENV secrets in Dockerfile)
gaps: Documentation lives in docs/security.md rather than a dedicated docs/secret-management.md file named in the catalogue — content is present.

evidence: No 0.0.0.0 default in code: host defaults to 127.0.0.1 (settings.py:22-25)
Host is env-configurable (MCP_HOST); 0.0.0.0 is only set explicitly in the Dockerfile (Dockerfile ENV MCP_HOST=0.0.0.0)
Binding all interfaces logs a warning at startup (__main__.py:42-51); README explains the local vs container distinction
All tool arguments carry pydantic Field schemas: CantonCode/IsoDate/SchoolTypeCode/Language/Include patterns (server.py:103-110)
Numeric inputs bounded: Year ge/le MIN_YEAR/MAX_YEAR, min_days ge=1 le=90, count ge=1 le=20 (server.py:107, 860, 878)
Patterns are whitelist-based (regex), not blacklist; unknown cantons rejected against the 26-code set (server.py:146-153)
test_tools.py exercises validation/edge cases; invalid input returns an error result, not a crash
gaps: strict=True / extra='forbid' are not explicitly set on the tool-input models; the comprehensive Field patterns and range bounds provide equivalent defense-in-depth.

evidence: docs/security.md 'Lethal-trifecta assessment' documents the evaluation
Only the 'private-data' leg exists, and only in the weakest sense (public data); no write/send leg — all tools are read-only (server.py:95-100)
No untrusted-instruction ingestion that could be acted upon; the trifecta is not present
gaps: The assessment text still says 'all 10 tools' (now 13) — count drift only; the read-only conclusion is unchanged since all 13 tools share the read-only annotation.

evidence: No os.system/os.popen/eval/exec/subprocess/shell=True anywhere in src/ (verified: grep -rnE over src/ → none)
No shell-outs at all; the only external interaction is httpx GETs with validated params
Requests carry timeouts (client.py:43, REQUEST_TIMEOUT/PROBE_TIMEOUT) guarding against resource exhaustion
Code-layer allow-list: immutable frozenset ALLOWED_HOSTS + pre-request assert_host_allowed (constants.py, client.py, guard.py)
Network-layer now shipped: deploy/cilium-egress-fqdn.yaml (Cilium toFQDNs) and deploy/networkpolicy.yaml (stock K8s fallback)
docs/network-egress.md describes the two-layer model and requires BOTH layers to be updated when the allow-list changes; referenced from README + security.md
